coredata.dc:title: A novel electrocatalyst based on NiO@C-dot nanocomposites for sensitive determination of ophthalmic drugs

Hafez, Ali
coredata.dc:description: This study proposed a novel electrochemical sensor for the sensitive measurement of the eye medication ofloxacin (OFX), which is based on a NiO@C-dot nanocomposite. The NiO@C-dot nanocomposite was synthesized using the sol-gel method, which was then applied to alter a glassy carbon electrode (GCE). With regard to OFX, the modified GCE shown outstanding electrochemical activity along with good sensitivity, selectivity, and stability. With OFX concentrations ranging from 5 µM to 975 µM, the NiO@C-dot/GCE showed a linear relationship with a sensitivity of 0.63994 µA/µM. The limit of detection (LOD) was 0.023 µM. The response to 50 µM OFX was measured, and the electrode was stored at 4°C after each test in order to evaluate the sensor's long-term stability and repeatability. After 20 days, the peak current response was still 97.89 % of its initial value. After 40 days of prolonged storage, 93.78 % of the electrode's original responsiveness was retained. The sensor showed a recovery rate of 97.37–99.70 % in all sample types when it was used to detect OFX in human urine, tap water, and food samples. These findings highlight the NiO@C-dot/GCE's outstanding selectivity, dependability, and longevity as solid platforms for OFX detection in complicated samples for a range of applications.

head.ce:title: A novel electrocatalyst based on NiO@C-dot nanocomposites for sensitive determination of ophthalmic drugs

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study proposed a novel electrochemical sensor for the sensitive measurement of the eye medication ofloxacin (OFX), which is based on a NiO@C-dot nanocomposite. The NiO@C-dot nanocomposite was synthesized using the sol-gel method, which was then applied to alter a glassy carbon electrode (GCE). With regard to OFX, the modified GCE shown outstanding electrochemical activity along with good sensitivity, selectivity, and stability. With OFX concentrations ranging from 5 µM to 975 µM, the NiO@C-dot/GCE showed a linear relationship with a sensitivity of 0.63994 µA/µM. The limit of detection (LOD) was 0.023 µM. The response to 50 µM OFX was measured, and the electrode was stored at 4°C after each test in order to evaluate the sensor's long-term stability and repeatability. After 20 days, the peak current response was still 97.89 % of its initial value. After 40 days of prolonged storage, 93.78 % of the electrode's original responsiveness was retained. The sensor showed a recovery rate of 97.37–99.70 % in all sample types when it was used to detect OFX in human urine, tap water, and food samples. These findings highlight the NiO@C-dot/GCE's outstanding selectivity, dependability, and longevity as solid platforms for OFX detection in complicated samples for a range of applications.

ce:section.ce:para: A critical component of clinical diagnostics, environmental monitoring, and pharmaceutical research is the identification of medications and their metabolites in biological and environmental samples [1]. Especially, because ophthalmic medications are used to treat a variety of eye disorders and diseases, their selection is crucial. Precise measurement of these medications guarantees medicinal effectiveness, tracks drug misuse, and facilitates comprehension of pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics. It is impossible to exaggerate how crucial this procedure is for patient safety, treatment efficacy, and general knowledge of how drugs behave in the environment and in human bodies.

Often used to treat bacterial infections, including those affecting the eyes, ofloxacin (OFX) is a second-generation fluoroquinolone antibiotic [2,3]. It functions by blocking the enzymes topoisomerase IV and bacterial DNA gyrase, which are essential for transcription, recombination, replication, and repair of bacterial DNA [4]. OFX has potential side effects, including nausea, diarrhea, and, in rare instances, tendon rupture, despite its therapeutic benefits [5]. As a result, determining OFX is essential for environmental surveillance, therapeutic drug monitoring, and drug misuse detection. Because OFX is widely used and has the potential to bioaccumulate, its significance goes beyond patient care and includes environmental impact studies [6].

Drug determination has been the subject of research using a variety of techniques, such as chromatographic [7,8], spectroscopic [9,10], and electrochemical methods [11–14]. Present-day techniques for determining OFX sometimes entail difficult and drawn-out processes like spectrophotometry and chromatography. Some locations may not be suitable for these procedures due to the need for specific equipment and qualified personnel. Furthermore, a few of these techniques might have poor selectivity or sensitivity, which could result in inaccurate OFX quantification in complicated samples. Thus, it is very desired to develop sensitive, selective, and economical technologies for OFX detection, especially for use in drug quality control, clinical settings, and environmental monitoring. Among them, electrochemical techniques have drawn attention due to their affordability, ease of use, and great sensitivity [15–18]. Electrochemical sensors provide real-time analysis, require less sample preparation than other techniques, and may operate with turbid and coloured materials [19–21]. Additionally, they are quite flexible, making it possible to detect a variety of analytes [22–24]. Electrochemical methods are the method of choice for drug determination in many sectors because of their efficiency and adaptability [25–27].

Recent years have seen a considerable increase in interest in the development of electrochemical sensors for the sensitive and selective detection of ophthalmic medicines because of their potential uses in environmental monitoring, clinical diagnostics, and drug quality control [28–30]. Numerous investigations have looked at the creation of electrochemical sensors for OFX detection using a variety of nanomaterials, such as metal oxides [13], carbon nanomaterials [14], and hybrid composites [23]. For example, Solangi et al. [13] developed a sensitive electrochemical sensor based on NiCo2O4 nanoboulder modification of a glassy carbon electrode for OFX detection. The application of graphene oxide-ionic liquids nanocomposite for the electrochemical determination of OFX was demonstrated by Wong et al. [15], exhibiting improved selectivity and sensitivity. Even though these studies have produced encouraging findings, more work needs to be done to create stable, highly sensitive, and selective electrochemical sensors for the detection of OFX. These sensors must also be developed to overcome issues with low sensitivity, poor selectivity, and susceptibility to interference in other compounds give in complex samples.

The field of sensor technology has seen a revolution thanks to the application of nanostructures in electrochemical sensors. Because of their unique catalytic capabilities, high surface-to-volume ratio, and superior conductivity, nanostructures improve the sensitivity, selectivity, and stability of sensors [15–17,31]. Nickel Oxide (NiO) nanoparticles have robust catalytic activity and superior conductivity, making them attractive materials for electrochemical sensor applications [32–34]. They can improve the performance of the sensor by facilitating electron transfer and offering a high active site density for the electrochemical reaction [35–37]. Zero-dimensional carbon-based nanostructures known as carbon dots (C-dots) have superior chemical stability and electron-transfer efficiency [38]. Higher sensitivity and selectivity can be achieved by improving the electrode's conductivity with the adding C-dots [39]. This allows for improved electron transport between the analyte and electrode surface [40,41]. The benefits of both NiO nanoparticles and C-dots are combined in the NiO@C-dot nanocomposite, creating a synergetic effect that develops the performance of the electrochemical sensor [42–44]. Higher surface-area aimed at analyte adsorption and interaction because of the nanocomposite structure results in higher electrochemical activity [45–47]. Furthermore, in comparison to individual components, the NiO@C-dot nanocomposite might provide greater stability and durability, guaranteeing continuous catalytic activity through several cycles of electrochemical processes.

We provide a new NiO@C-dot nanocomposite in this work for the sensitive measurement of OFX. The NiO@C-dot nanocomposite offers a synergistic impact that improves the performance of the electrochemical sensor by combining the benefits of NiO nanoparticles and C-dots. A straightforward and affordable technique was utilized to create the nanocomposite, which was then applied to alter a glassy-carbon electrode (GCE). The complimentary features of NiO and C-dots led to their selection for our NiO@C-dot/GCE sensor. C-dots offer remarkable conductivity and a huge surface area for increased sensitivity, while NiO gives strong electrochemical activity and stability. The combination of these materials produces a synergistic effect that greatly enhances the performance of the sensor. Very selective and sensitive electrochemical sensors have been developed as a result of recent developments in carbon dot research [48]. Carbon dots' special qualities, namely their large surface area, adjustable surface functions, and superior electrical conductivity, have been used to improve sensor performance [49,50]. These advancements have produced nanocomposites such as our NiO@C-dot, which demonstrate exceptional sensitivity and selectivity for target analytes in intricate sample matrices.

With regard to OFX, the modified GCE shown outstanding electrochemical activity along with good sensitivity, selectivity, and stability. This work opens up new possibilities for the creation of effective electrochemical drug detection sensors. The effective production of the NiO@C-dot nanocomposite and its use in the sensitive determination of OFX, demonstrating its potential as a potent tool in pharmaceutical analysis and environmental monitoring, are what make this work novel. An important development in electrochemical sensing technology is the creation of the NiO@C-dot/GCE sensor. By combining the special qualities of carbon dots and nickel oxide nanoparticles, this innovative method creates a sensor that is extremely stable, economical, and environmentally benign. The NiO@C-dot nanocomposite is a potential instrument for pharmaceutical analysis and environmental monitoring because it improves sensitivity and selectivity while retaining good stability.

Sigma-Aldrich, USA provided the Glassy Carbon Electrode (GCE) with a diameter of 3 mm, α-Al2O3 powders (0.3 µm and 0.05 µm particle sizes, 99.99 % purity), Triton X-100 (C14H22O (C2H4O)n, 99 %), dopamine (≥99 %), and Tris-buffer. Merck, Germany provided the following products: 99 % pure ethanol, 98 % pure nickel (II) nitrate hexahydrate (Ni(NO3)2·6 H2O), 98 % pure isopropanol alcohol (CH3)2CHOH), 25 % pure ammonium hydroxide (NH4OH), and 99 % pure polyethylene glycol (H(OCH2CH2)nOH). Every chemical was pure enough to be utilized analytically, and it was used exactly as it was received. We used deionized water for the entire synthesis procedure. In order to prepare 0.1 M Phosphate-Buffered-Saline (PBS) as per electrolyte for electrochemical experiments, Sigma-Aldrich (USA) provided Monosodium Phosphate (NaH2PO4, 99.0 %) and Disodium Phosphate (Na2HPO4, 98 %). Hydrochloric acid (HCl, 37 %) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 99.0 %) were purchased from Merck in Germany. We bought KCl (99.0 %) and Fe(CN)6 3−/4− (99.0 %) from Sigma-Aldrich, USA.

The NiO@C-dot nanocomposite was synthesized using the sol-gel process, which was selected because of its versatility, controllability, and capacity to provide well-defined nanostructures. By precisely controlling the temperature, pressure, and reaction duration during the process, highly crystalline NiO nanoparticles with consistent size and shape can be formed. Furthermore, the sol-gel method minimizes the usage of harmful chemicals and lowers the overall environmental effect, making it a very easy and environmentally benign procedure.

To make NiO nanoparticles, 50 mg of nickel (II) nitrate hexahydrate were ultrasonically dissolved in 25 milliliters of isopropanol alcohol and 20 milliliters of polyethylene glycol [51]. Until the mixture was entirely dissolved, a magnetic stirrer was used to agitate it for the entire day. After that, ammonium hydroxide was added until the pH of the mixture reached 11. Triton X-100 was introduced at a concentration of 0.5 % (v/v) to stop particle clumping. After that, the liquids were gradually heated to 75 °C until a gel took shape. After drying at 200 °C, this gel was crushed into a powder.

In order to produce the NiO@C-dot nanocomposite, 100 mL of a Tris-buffer (pH 8.5) containing 0.6 mg/mL of the dopamine biomolecule was mixed with 40 mg of the synthesized NiO nanocrystals, and the mixture was then allowed to air dry [52]. After then, the polymerization process lasted for ten hours at room temperature. The resulting NiO nanoparticles coated with poly-dopamine were repeatedly cleaned in deionized water. The product was collected by filtration using basic filter paper after this procedure was carried out three times. To create the required NiO@C-dot nanocomposite, the poly-dopamine coated NiO nanoparticles were finally calcined at 400°C for 5 hours in an argon environment.

The modified GCE was polished with 1.0 μm, 0.3 μm, and 0.05 μm α-alumina slurry on polishing pad in that order to get a mirror-like finish. Before being used, it was air-dried and successively cleaned in an ultrasonic bath using distilled water, ethanol, and distilled water once more. 10.0 μL of 10 mg/mL NiO@C-dot nanocomposite mix solution was applied to the GCE's surface using a micro injector. After that, it was located in oven set at 50°C for 40 minutes to dry. Graphical representation of preparation electrode process is exhibited in Scheme 1.

Using a Bruker Advanced X-ray Solutions D9, XRD (X-ray Diffraction) was used to identify the phases and analyze the structure. The observations were made with monochromatized Cu Kα radiation(λ=1.5406 Å) in 2θ ranging 5° to 80°. Direct examination of the nanostructures' forms and structures was done utilizing a Zeiss SupraTM, 35VP FESEM (Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscopy). Additionally, a Thermo Fisher Scientific K-Alpha+ instrument was used to do XPS (X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy) investigation in order to look in the surface chemistry of the nanostructures. A PerkinElmer Spectrum Two FT-IR Spectrometer was also used to do FTIR (Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy) analysis in order to determine which functional groups were present in the sample. A CH Instruments Model 600E Series Electrochemical Analyzer/Workstation was used to perform the electrochemical measurements. Ag/AgCl (3 M KCl) was used as the reference electrode, platinum wire aided as the counter electrode, and modified or bare GCE served as working electrode in this three-electrode setup. A benchtop pH meter from Fristaden Lab was used to measure the pH values. Every experiment was carried out in natural settings.

The electrolyte used in all electrochemical tests was 0.1 M PBS. In this electrolyte, cyclic voltammetry (CV) measurements were performed at a scan rate of 50 mV/s. Tests using Differential Pulse Voltammetry (DPV) were led to a 50-mV pulse amplitude and a 0.05 s pulse width. Measurements of electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were conducted on both modified and naked GCE in a solution containing 0.1 M KCl and 10.0 mM Fe(CN)6 3-/4-. A 0.3 V applied potential and a frequency range of 105 Hz to 10−1 Hz were used in the EIS testing. The averages of four replicate measurements made using the three electrodes were included in the electrochemical results. The regularity and dependability of the outcomes are highlighted by this data.

An informed consent was obtained from a healthy volunteer before a human urine sample was prepared. For ten minutes, the sample was centrifuged at 4000 rpm to remove any solid particles. Earlier examination, supernatant was collected and diluted ten times with phosphate buffer solution (PBS, pH 6). Commercial orange juice was bought from a nearby supermarket and used to prepare the orange juice sample. Using 0.45 µm syringe-filter, orange juice was first purified to get rid of any pulp or solid particles. Before analysis, the filtered juice was diluted ten times with PBS (pH 6). Tap water was collected locally in order to prepare a sample of it. For the tap water sample, there was no need for dilution. To achieve adsorption-desorption equilibrium, the NiO@C-dot/GCE was dissolved in the sample solution and agitated for five minutes prior to the examination of every sample. Then, under the ideal circumstances, the electrochemical measurements were completed. The conventional addition technique was utilized to calculate the concentration of OFX in each genuine sample. The samples were mixed by known volumes of OFX standard solution, and the rise in the current response was recorded.

The SEM picture, as shown in Fig. 1a, shows that C-dots adhere uniformly to the surface of NiO NPs while maintaining the globular structure of the NiO NPs. On the other hand, Fig. 1a's nanocomposite particles display a lack of homogeneity and clumping in irregular shapes. Furthermore, there is more proof of the development of C-dots on the surface of NiO NPs in Fig. 1b's TEM image. These results point to a nuanced interaction between the nanocomposite's C-dots and NiO NPs.

The XRD patterns of C-dots,NiO NPs, and the NiO@C-dot nanocomposite is revealed in Fig. 2a. The presence of a turbostratic carbon phase is shown by the single, broad diffraction peak centered at 2θ = 20.96° in the XRD pattern of C-dots. These results generally agree with those from earlier research [53,54]. The NiO nanoparticles' XRD investigation shows peaks at angles of 37.25°, 43.61°, 62.79°, 75.32°, and 79.44⁰ that correspond to particular crystal planes. These peaks are in good agreement with the NiO nanoparticles' FCC (Face Centred Cubic) crystalline Bunsenite structure, as shown by JCPDS Card No. 04–0835. These summits are chosen as (111), (200), (220), (311), and (222), in that order [55,56]. The XRD of NiO@C-dot nanocomposite indicates that the synthesis of the nanocomposite was successful since it displays a diffraction peak at 20.96° that corresponds to C-dots. Other diffraction peaks in the pattern also line up with those of NiO.

The FTIR of C-dots,NiO NPs,and the NiO@C-dot nanocomposite is shown in Fig. 2b. The peaks found in the FTIR spectra of C-dots at 3434, 1646, 1534, and 1208 cm−1 correspond to the functional groups that are existing in the C-dots, which are OH, COOH, CO, and C–O–C [55,57,58], respectively. A Ni–O stretching vibration mode is visible in the 400–600 cm−1 range of the NiO NPs' FTIR spectra [59,60]. Metal oxides are known for their interatomic vibrations, which are shown by absorption bands below 613 cm−1 [61,62]. The O–H stretching vibration of the water molecules within the interlayer is responsible for a wide absorption band that peaks at 3312 cm−1 [63,64]. Strong, thin bands at 1094 cm−1, 1384 cm−1, 1560 cm−1, and 2097 cm−1 are indicative of para-substituted aromatic rings, C–O stretching, CH3 stretching, and acid group CO stretching [65–67], respectively. These are probably brought on by the employment of polyethylene glycol and isopropanol alcohol in the synthesis process. The primary peaks of both C-dots and NiO NPs are seen in the FTIR spectrum of the NiO@C-dot nanocomposite, showing that both components were successfully combined.

An extensive view of the NiO@C-dot nanocomposite's XPS spectrum is shown in Fig. 3. Fig. 3a shows the nanocomposite in its whole range. The XPS spectrum of Ni2p is revealed in Fig. 3b, where a satellite peak is produced by the electrons moving from the ligand to Nickel when it is exposed to X-ray radiation. A peak at a binding-energy of 855.8 eV and its neighboring satellite peak suggest the presence of 2p3/2 peaks of NiO [68,69]. Similarly, with a binding energy of 873.1 eV, a peak and its nearby satellite peaks resemble 2p1/2 peak NiO [68,69]. Consistent with the XRD results, this evidence points to the presence of NiO NPs in the produced nanocomposite, displaying a full lattice structure. The XPS spectra of O1s is shown in Fig. 3c, where NiO and H2O are represented by two separate peaks. The XPS spectra of C 1 s is shown in Fig. 3d. It displays three-distinct peaks in 284.5 eV, 286.6 eV, and 288.4 eV that are related to CC,C-O,and O-CO bonds [70,71], respectively. It is clear from the results of FTIR, SEM, TEM, XRD, and XPS that the NiO@C-dot nanocomposite has been synthesized successfully.

Using a [Fe(CN6)]3-/4- pair as a redox probe, the electrochemical properties related to the electron-transfer of GCE,NiO/GCE, C-dot/GCE, and NiO@C-dot/GCE were studied (as seen in Fig. 4a). EIS techniques were used to an electrolyte solution containing 0.1 M KCl in addition 10 mM [Fe(CN6)]3-/4-. The electrode surface can be understood by variations in the impedance value. Nyquist plots based on the Randles equivalent circuit—which is displayed in the inset of Fig. 4a—are fitted to perform the EIS analysis. This includes a low-frequency linear pattern and a high-frequency semicircular pattern, which represent the processes of diffusion and electron transport, respectively. The semicircle's diameter and the material's charge transfer resistance (Rct) match [72,73]. When the four curves consistent with NiO@C-dot/GCE are compared, it can be seen that the strongest reduction and the fastest interfacial electron transfer occur in the smallest semicircle, which corresponds to Rct = 1.09 kΩ. It suggests that the electrode contact is experiencing easier electron transport. Because it allows more effective reduction processes, this is advantageous for electrochemical reactions. On the other hand, the impedance value of the GCE exhibits the slowest interfacial electron transfer, with the greatest Rct value (2.68 kΩ).

Using cyclic voltammetry, the effectiveness of the modified electrodes was evaluated. The peak separation values (∼ΔEp) for [Fe(CN6)]3-/4- exhibiting a CV response were 0.28, 0.25, 0.26, and 0.11 V for GCE, NiO/GCE, C-dot/GCE, and NiO@C-dot/GCE, as shown in Fig. 4a. This displays that the NiO@C-dot and the GCE are effectively communicating electronically. Using Randles-Sevcik equation, cyclic voltammograms were utilized to calculate the electrochemical-active surface-area(A) of the NiO@C-dot/GCE [74]:

In this case, n is the electron count, D presents the diffusion-coefficient(7.6×10−6 cm2/s), v presents the scan-rate(V/s), and C shows the probe concentration(mol/cm3). Ip stands for the redox peak current (A). For GCE, NiO/GCE, C-dot/GCE, and NiO@C-dot/GCE, the computed values of A are 0.015, 0.051, 0.068, and 0.085 cm2, in that order.

The electrochemical reactions of 50 µM OFX on GCE, NiO/GCE, C-dot/GCE, and NiO@C-dot/GCE in 0.1 M PBS are shown in Fig. 5a. On GCE, the oxidation current is smaller, whereas on NiO/GCE and C-dot/GCE, larger peak currents are seen. Interestingly, NiO@C-dot/GCE shows a significant peak current increase, indicating superior OFX oxidation catalytic efficiency. As seen in Fig. 5b, a potential electrochemical reaction was suggested for the oxidation of ofloxacin that involved the piperazine motion oxidation with the release of two electrons and two protons [21]. The ambient oxygen in the electrolyte solution, which takes part in the electrochemical reactions at the sensor surface, is the source of oxygen in the oxidation mechanism. A synergistic effect between the NiO NPs and C-dots on the electrode surface improves electron transfer kinetics and increases catalytic activity for the oxidation of OFX [75]. More active sites are available for the electrochemical reaction when both NiO and C-dots are present, which makes the oxidation of OFX molecules more effective. Higher peak currents are the outcome of enhanced electron transport between the electrode surface and the OFX molecules made possible by the addition of C-dots, which also improves the electrode's conductivity [76]. In accordance with SEM and TEM data, the NiO@C-dot nanocomposite structure probably offers a greater surface area for OFX adsorption and interaction, resulting in higher peak currents and improved electrochemical activity. Furthermore, compared to individual components, the NiO@C-dot nanocomposite may provide greater stability and durability, guaranteeing maintained catalytic activity throughout several cycles of electrochemical reactions. According to EIS analyses, the combination of NiO and C-dots may promote faster electron-transfer during oxidation of OFX and reduce resistance at electrode-electrolyte interface, hence facilitating efficient charge transfer processes. When compared to individual components or other modified electrodes, the NiO@C-dot nanocomposite modified electrode exhibits a significantly higher peak current, suggesting its improved catalytic activity aimed at the electrochemical oxidation of OFX. Consequently, after doing electrochemical research on NiO@C-dot/GCE. Furthermore, it is noted that the peak position and shape did not vary noticeably with modification steps; rather, they remained constant. The higher current is therefore believed to be caused by the NiO@C-dot nanocomposite's larger surface area. If substitute materials demonstrate acceptable electrochemical characteristics for OFX detection, they may also be employed. These materials have large effective surface areas.

Since protons are involved in the electrochemical oxidation of OFX, the effect of pH on this process must be investigated. The study of OFX oxidation in PBS at different pH values (3.5–9) with 50 µM OFX utilizing CV analysis is shown in Fig. 6a. The findings show that peak currents rise with increasing pH up to 6.0 and then decrease after that. The ideal supporting electrolyte was therefore determined to be pH 6.0. The pH of the supporting electrolyte has an effect on the peak current variations shown in Fig. 6a. Higher peak currents up to pH 6.0 result from an electrochemical environment that is more conducive to OFX oxidation as pH rises. Peak currents drop as the environment becomes less favorable after this point. This behavior is in line with OFX's interaction with the sensor surface and its protonation state. The relationship between pH and oxidation potentials (Ep) is shown in Fig. 6b by the following equation: Ep (V)=1.21098 (V/pH) - 0.05609 pH (R2 = 0.99861). According to this equation, the proton-transfer quantity is 2, that is according to the mechanism shown in Fig. 5b [77]. These findings are based on the Nernst equation, which predicts that a two-proton transfer mechanism will have a slope of about 59 mV/pH at ambient temperature. A two-proton transfer mechanism is indicated by the observed slope value of 56 mV/pH in our investigation, which is in close proximity to this theoretical value.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Voltametric determination and calibration curve of OFX
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 7 shows the calibration curve and the DPV response to different OFX concentrations. A linear association between the NiO@C-dot/GCE and OFX concentrations ranging from 5 µM to 975 µM was observed. Ip (µA) = −0.93596 + 0.1289 C (µM) was the linear regression equation; it had a sensitivity of 0.63994 µA/µM and a high correlation coefficient of 0.99911. The correctness of the calibration curve is supported by the high correlation coefficient of 0.997, which indicates a strong link between concentration and current response. The electrode's ability to detect even minute levels of OFX is demonstrated by its determined limit of detection (LOD), which stands at 0.023 µM. Using the formula 3.3σ/S, the LOD was calculated by taking the slope of the calibration curve (S) and the standard deviation of the blank sample (σ). This makes the electrode an invaluable analytical instrument. Table 1 also includes a list of other modified electrodes that were tested. The results indicate that NiO@C-dot/GCE is a potentially useful platform for OFX electrochemical detection. Our NiO@C-dot/GCE sensor's increased sensitivity and selectivity are explained by the combination of NiO's catalytic qualities and carbon dots' high surface area and good electron transport capabilities. Better interactions between ofloxacin molecules and this combination result in enhanced detection capabilities.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The impact of the scan rate
ce:section.ce:para: We investigated the effect of the scan rate by using a concentration of 50 µM OFX. The responses of the detector to a range of possible scan speeds (10–200 mV/s) were recorded ( Fig. 8a). The cyclic voltammetric results showed that the OFX oxidation peak currents gradually rose with scan rate. In addition, it was found that the peak current and scan rate had a linear connection, which can be expressed as follows: Ipa(A)= 4.0771+40.4211 v (R2=0.99819), as shown in Fig. 8b. According to these findings, the NiO@C-dot Nanocomposites, via an adsorption-controlled process, are essential for improving electron transport at GCE. This result highlights the effective kinetics of electron transport made possible by the nanocomposites, enhancing the system's overall electrochemical performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study the stability, repeatability, and reproducibility of NiO@C-dot/GCE
ce:section.ce:para: By monitoring the response to 50 µM OFX, modified electrode's long-term stability and repeatability were evaluated. After each test, the electrode was kept at 4°C. It is clear from Fig. 9a that after 20 days, the peak current response held steady at 97.89 % of its initial value. Excellent long-term stability and repeatability were indicated by the electrode, which retained 93.78 % of its initial response even after being stored for 40 days. Six electrodes were made for the purpose of measuring 10 mM OFX under the same conditions in order to assess repeatability. Strong repeatability was indicated by the relative standard deviation (RSD), which was found to be 2.66 % (Fig. 9b). The durable design and composition of NiO@C-dot/GCE, which successfully maintains its electrochemical properties over time, is responsible for the strange long-term stability and repeatability of the modified electrode. The electrode's resistance to external conditions is demonstrated by its steady response, even after extended storage at 4°C. Moreover, the accurate fabrication process that guaranteed electrode uniformity and produced accurate and consistent results across several experiments is accountable for the good reproducibility in the sensor's performance that has been observed.

By simultaneously detecting the target molecule and interferents, the selectivity of the sensor was assessed. The results showed that the detection of OFX was unaffected by a 5-fold content of Zn2+,Mg2+,Na+,K+,Al3+,Ca2+,Fe2+,Cl-, ascorbic acid (AA), uric acid (US), glucose, urea, and phenol. The possibility of their interference was also observed into because moxifloxacin (MXF), gatifloxacin (GFX), and ciprofloxacin (CFX) share structural similarities with OFX. As shown in Fig. 10, MXF, GXF, and CFX have little effect on OFX detection. The NiO@C-dot/GCE sensor's remarkable selectivity can be ascribed to its distinct molecular recognition characteristics, which allow it to discern OFX from a variety of possible interferents. It is further confirmed that the sensor can identify OFX with high selectivity because structurally related substances like MXF, GXF, and CFX do not interfere with its detection. In order to ensure precise and dependable target molecule detection, cross-reactivity with other chemicals is probably prevented in large part by the electrode's composition and design. These findings highlight the NiO@C-dot/GCE's outstanding selectivity, dependability, and longevity as a solid platform for OFX detection in complicated samples for a range of applications. The molecular recognition capabilities of the NiO@C-dot/GCE sensor is responsible for its selectivity. NiO nanoparticles' carbon dots offer a large number of active sites and certain functional groups that connect with OFX molecules only. Furthermore, the sensor's electrochemical optimization enables accurate OFX detection free from interference from related substances.

The sensor was used to find OFX in food samples, tap water, and human urine. Table 2 shows that the approach is reliable and effective, with recovery rates ranging from 97.37 % to 99.70 % across all sample categories. Furthermore, the RSD was less than 4.60 %, demonstrating remarkable accuracy.

To sum up, our work successfully synthesized a novel NiO@C-dot nanocomposite and illustrated how to use it as an electrochemical sensor to detect the ophthalmic medication OFX with high sensitivity. By combining the benefits of C-dots and NiO nanoparticles, the NiO@C-dot nanocomposite greatly improved the glassy carbon electrode's (GCE) performance. With regard to OFX, the modified GCE showed outstanding electrochemical activity along with good sensitivity, selectivity, and stability. With OFX concentrations ranging from 5 µM to 975 µM, the NiO@C-dot/GCE demonstrated a strong linear connection with a high correlation-coefficient of 0.99911 and sensitivity of 0.63994 µA/µM. The sensor indicated the ability to detect traces of OFX, as evidenced by the remarkably low limit of detection (LOD) of 0.023 µM. Additionally, after 40 days of storage at 4°C, the sensor showed exceptional long-term stability and repeatability, retaining over 93 % of its initial response. Additionally, the sensor had a recovery rate of 97.37–99.70 % in all sample types when used to detect OFX in complex samples such human urine, tap water, and food samples. These findings highlight NiO@C-dot nanocomposite's potential as an effective instrument for environmental monitoring and pharmaceutical investigation. The results of this work provide fresh opportunities for the creation of effective electrochemical drug detection sensors, advancing the domains of environmental monitoring, clinical diagnostics, and pharmaceutical research. It is crucial to recognize certain limitations even though our investigation shows the NiO@C-dot/GCE has great potential for sensitive and trustworthy OFX detection in a variety of sample matrices. It is necessary to conduct additional research to determine the sensor's long-term stability in practical settings and how well it functions when biological samples contain additional possible interferents. Furthermore, a worthwhile avenue for future study would be to investigate whether downsizing the sensor for point-of-care applications is feasible. Further enhancing this approach's practical utility could be investigating its application for the detection of other ophthalmic medications. The NiO@C-dot/GCE sensor created in this work has excellent potential for real-world applications due to its selectivity, sensitivity, and stability. Drug quality control, clinical diagnostics, and environmental monitoring are some of its possible uses. In order to evaluate possible environmental pollution, the sensor may be used to find OFX residues in environmental samples including soil and wastewater.
Zhao Chenjin: Writing – original draft, Validation, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology. Ali Hafez: Writing – original draft, Resources, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis.
